metadata.title: D003: Implement infrastructure deployment automation E2E

summary: Second review after all 6 issues from review 1 were addressed. The implementation is complete and well-structured: all Makefile targets replace stubs correctly, shell scripts follow project conventions (SPDX, set -euo pipefail, log(), CC-0010 references, configurable timeouts), kustomize overlays correctly patch production manifests without modification, the Chainsaw E2E test covers all 12 required assertions, the CI e2e-infra job uses SHA-pinned actions with diagnostic dump on failure, and install-test-deps.sh now verifies SHA256 checksums for all downloaded binaries. All review-1 blockers resolved: chainsaw installation added to CI, checksum verification implemented, unnecessary secret deletion removed, BAO_TOKEN unset after bootstrap, jq --arg used for safe interpolation, PATH documented in Quick Start. No blocking or major issues remain.

tests_checklist.item: Test covers all 12 required assertions (4 operator Deployments, 1 StatefulSet, ClusterIssuer, MariaDB CR, Memcached CR, ClusterSecretStore, 3 ExternalSecrets)
Test uses extended assert timeout (5m) for operator startup
Test uses v1alpha2 API version matching Chainsaw v0.3+ requirement
CI e2e-infra job installs chainsaw via make install-test-deps and adds to PATH

code_quality_checklist.item: Shell scripts follow SPDX Apache-2.0 header convention
Shell scripts include CC-0010 feature reference
log() function uses ISO 8601 UTC timestamps matching existing pattern
Functions have single responsibility (preflight, wait_for_*, init, bootstrap)
Wait functions have configurable timeouts via environment variables
No dead code or commented-out code

security_checklist.item: No hardcoded secrets in scripts or configs
Unseal keys passed via stdin (not as command-line arguments)
BAO_TOKEN unset after bootstrap phase completes (line 372)
Downloaded binaries verified with SHA256 checksums from upstream releases
Production manifests not modified (kind-specific changes in deploy/kind/ only)
CI job uses permissions: contents: read (least privilege)
All CI action references SHA-pinned with version comments

architecture_checklist.item: Two-phase kustomize pattern correctly applied (base then infrastructure)
Kustomize overlays reference production manifests without modification
OpenBao bootstrap scripts invoked as-is from CC-0009
SKIP_KIND_CREATE flag enables CI mode where cluster is pre-created
Solution is as simple as possible for the requirements

dry_yagni_checklist.item: No features beyond what was explicitly requested

fail_fast_checklist.item: Preflight checks verify all required CLI tools before deployment
Preflight checks verify Docker is running
Preflight checks detect existing kind cluster
Wait functions timeout with descriptive error messages and diagnostic dump
Bootstrap scripts validated as executable before invocation
SHA256 checksum verification aborts on mismatch with clear error

defensive_checklist.item: All external inputs (env vars) have sensible defaults
Teardown script is idempotent (succeeds if no cluster exists)
install-test-deps skips tools already at correct version
Wait loops have deadlines preventing infinite hangs

issues_found.title: Same-origin checksum verification provides limited supply-chain protection
issues_found.description: install-test-deps.sh downloads SHA256 checksums from the same GitHub Release as the binaries (e.g., checksums.txt alongside the tarball). If an attacker compromises the GitHub Release page, both the binary and its checksum file are replaced simultaneously. The verification protects against transport corruption and CDN cache poisoning but not against a compromised upstream. This is standard practice for CI tooling scripts and acceptable for an E2E testing context.
issues_found.fix: For stronger supply-chain guarantees, pin expected SHA256 hashes as constants in the script (one per platform per tool) so they become part of code review. Example: CHAINSAW_SHA256_linux_amd64='abc123...'. This is a hardening suggestion, not a blocker.

issues_found.title: Memcached operator Deployment name is unverified assumption
issues_found.description: The Chainsaw test at line 68 asserts on Deployment name 'memcached-operator-controller-manager' based on kubebuilder convention. A DECISION comment at lines 57-62 documents this assumption. The actual Helm chart Deployment name should be verified after first successful deployment.
issues_found.fix: After first successful end-to-end run, verify the actual Deployment name with 'kubectl get deploy -n memcached-system' and update the assertion if needed.

issues_found.title: NAMESPACE vs OPENBAO_NAMESPACE variable naming is potentially confusing
issues_found.description: deploy-infra.sh reads NAMESPACE from the environment (line 41) and stores it in OPENBAO_NAMESPACE for its own use. The child bootstrap scripts read NAMESPACE from common.sh. When both variables are unset, defaults align correctly (both default to 'openbao-system'). When NAMESPACE is set in the environment, it is inherited by child processes correctly. However, the internal variable rename to OPENBAO_NAMESPACE could confuse future maintainers who might try to set OPENBAO_NAMESPACE directly (which would not propagate to bootstrap scripts).
issues_found.fix: Add a comment on line 41 explaining that NAMESPACE (not OPENBAO_NAMESPACE) is the user-facing environment variable, and that it is inherited by bootstrap scripts via common.sh.

suggested_improvements: After first successful deployment, verify the memcached-operator Deployment name (chainsaw-test.yaml:68) and update if it differs from the kubebuilder convention assumption
Consider adding shellcheck CI validation for hack/*.sh scripts to catch future shell scripting issues automatically
For stronger supply-chain protection, consider pinning expected SHA256 hashes as constants rather than fetching them from the same origin as the binaries
next_steps: Merge the feature branch — all blocking and major issues are resolved
Verify memcached-operator Deployment name after first successful E2E run in CI

detected_patterns.name: Infrastructure deployment script with 8-step orchestration and configurable timeouts
detected_patterns.applies_when: Adding a new deployment orchestration script that deploys multiple interdependent components to a kind cluster with health-wait gates between phases
detected_patterns.description: Deployment scripts follow a numbered step sequence with log() delimiters ('=== Step N/M: description ==='), wait_for_* helper functions with configurable timeouts via environment variables, preflight checks for required CLI tools and Docker, and a SKIP_*_CREATE flag for CI mode where the cluster is pre-created by a GitHub Action. Each wait function polls every 10 seconds with a deadline, dumps diagnostic info on timeout, and exits 1. The script uses set -euo pipefail, SPDX Apache-2.0 header, and feature ID reference.

examples.code:
wait_for_helmreleases() {
  local timeout="$1"
  shift
  local releases=("$@")
  local deadline=$(( $(date +%s) + timeout ))

  log "Waiting up to ${timeout}s for HelmReleases to become Ready: ${releases[*]}"

  while true; do
    local all_ready=true
    for release in "${releases[@]}"; do
      local ns
      ns=$(kubectl get helmrelease --all-namespaces -o json 2>/dev/null \
        | jq -r --arg name "${release}" '.items[] | select(.metadata.name == $name) | .metadata.namespace' 2>/dev/null) || true
      ...
    done
    if [[ $(date +%s) -ge ${deadline} ]]; then
      log "ERROR: Timed out waiting for HelmReleases after ${timeout}s."
      kubectl get helmrelease --all-namespaces 2>/dev/null || true
      exit 1
    fi
    sleep 10
  done
}

detected_patterns.name: Binary installation with version-skip and SHA256 checksum verification
detected_patterns.applies_when: Adding a script that downloads and installs CLI tool binaries from GitHub Releases or upstream URLs for development/CI use
detected_patterns.description: Each install_* function follows a consistent pattern: (1) check if the tool is already installed at the correct version (skip if so), (2) download the binary to a temporary directory, (3) download the upstream checksum file, (4) verify SHA256 checksum via a shared verify_sha256 helper, (5) install with 0755 permissions. The temporary directory is cleaned up via trap RETURN. Version detection uses tool-specific version commands with grep -oP for version extraction. Platform detection (OS/ARCH) is centralized in a detect_platform function.

examples.code:
verify_sha256() {
  local file="$1"
  local expected="$2"
  if [[ -z "${expected}" ]]; then
    log "ERROR: Empty expected SHA256 hash for $(basename "${file}")."
    exit 1
  fi
  local actual
  actual=$(sha256sum "${file}" | awk '{print $1}')
  if [[ "${actual}" != "${expected}" ]]; then
    log "ERROR: SHA256 checksum mismatch for $(basename "${file}")"
    log "  expected: ${expected}"
    log "  actual:   ${actual}"
    exit 1
  fi
  log "  SHA256 checksum verified."
}

install_chainsaw() {
  local target="${INSTALL_DIR}/chainsaw"
  local want="${CHAINSAW_VERSION}"
  if [[ -x "${target}" ]]; then
    local got
    got="$("${target}" version 2>/dev/null | grep -oP 'v[\d.]+' | head -1)" || true
    if [[ "${got}" == "${want}" ]]; then
      log "chainsaw ${want} already installed — skipping."
      return
    fi
  fi
  log "Installing chainsaw ${want}..."
  local url="https://github.com/kyverno/chainsaw/releases/download/${want}/chainsaw_${OS}_${ARCH}.tar.gz"
  local tmpdir
  tmpdir="$(mktemp -d)"
  trap 'rm -rf "${tmpdir}"' RETURN
  curl -fsSL "${url}" -o "${tmpdir}/chainsaw.tar.gz"
  local checksums_url="https://github.com/kyverno/chainsaw/releases/download/${want}/checksums.txt"
  curl -fsSL "${checksums_url}" -o "${tmpdir}/checksums.txt"
  local expected_hash
  expected_hash=$(grep "chainsaw_${OS}_${ARCH}.tar.gz" "${tmpdir}/checksums.txt" | awk '{print $1}')
  verify_sha256 "${tmpdir}/chainsaw.tar.gz" "${expected_hash}"
  tar -xzf "${tmpdir}/chainsaw.tar.gz" -C "${tmpdir}" chainsaw
  install -m 0755 "${tmpdir}/chainsaw" "${target}"
  log "chainsaw ${want} installed to ${target}."
}
